Title: GRADE Guidance 44: strategies to enhance the utilization of randomized and non-randomized studies in evidence syntheses of health interventions

Abstract: Background and Objectives: Ideally, guideline developers and health technology assessment authors base intervention decisions on randomized controlled trials (RCTs). However, relying solely on RCTs is uncommon, especially for public health interventions and harms assessment. In these situations, nonrandomized studies of interventions (NRSIs) can provide valuable information. This article presents Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE) guidance for integrating bodies of evidence RCT and NRSI in evidence syntheses of health interventions. Methods: Following standard GRADE methods, we developed this guidance through iterative discussions and examples with experts from the GRADE NRSI project group in multiple dedicated meetings. We presented findings of the group discussions for feedback at GRADE Working Group meetings in September 2023 and May 2024. Results: The resulting GRADE guidance outlines a structured approach: (1) assessing the certainty of evidence (CoE) after defining the number of decision thresholds and the target of the certainty rating; (2) evaluating congruency of effect estimates between RCTs and NRSIs; (3) identifying which GRADE domains are affected by certainty ratings to inform complementariness between RCTs and NRSIs and the overall CoE; and (4) deciding whether and how to use one or both types of studies. Funding: Part of this work has been presented in scientific conferences and at GRADE working group meetings and Cochrane symposia. No financial conflicts directly related to this paper exist. Background for this work has been obtained through Surveys and discussions within the GRADE NRSI/RCTs integration working group. documents.page_content:
Introduction
High-certainty evidence from randomized controlled trials (RCTs) allows for confident conclusions from evidence syntheses assessing health interventions. Well-conducted RCTs, ie, those with an adequate design, number of representative participants, follow-up, measure of outcomes, etc, will yield effect estimates that are less susceptible to bias and other sources of uncertainty procuring better opportunities to answer questions of causal effects of interventions [1]. These instances are desirable but remain relatively rare and may explain why a sizable proportion of the research dedicated to health interventions relies on observational evidence [2][3][4].
Evidence syntheses of health interventions, whether these are standalone evidence synthesis or used to inform health technology assessments (HTAs) or health care guidelines, include evidence comprised of RCTs, nonrandomized studies of interventions (NRSIs), or a combination of both.
Previous Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE) publications have examined the use of RCTs and NRSI in evidence synthesis, including the rating of the certainty of evidence (CoE) in NRSIs and the use of specific risk of bias (RoB) tools [5] authors' practices and preferences, [6] and case studies exploring differences between RCTs and NRSI [7]. This body of work informed earlier GRADE guidance on when NRSI should be considered alongside RCTs in syntheses addressing interventions [8,9]. GRADE users who consider utilizing both RCTs and NRSIs to inform a given question require guidance on how to effectively integrate evidence from these 2 distinct study designs. This official guidance aims to support such integration by providing structured principles to evaluate when and how combining RCT and NRSI evidence is appropriate within the GRADE approach.

documents.page_content:
Operationalization of guidance
We examined methods for evidence rating, result presentation, and the GRADE domains most affected by integrating RCTs and NRSIs. Building on prior work on author attitudes toward pooling these studies, we conducted in-depth discussions and multiple virtual meetings, including 2 full-day sessions.
We identified key examples to guide discussions on when to synthesize RCT and NRSI evidence. We defined scenarios for using NRSIs as replacement, complementary, or sequential evidence in certainty ratings. Recognizing the importance of effect estimate similarity, we focused on defining congruency between RCTs and NRSIs and its impact on deciding which evidence to use and how to rate the certainty [8,9].
We summarized the results for the relevant GRADE project group and presented the draft and final guidance to the full GRADE Working Group twice in September 2022 (40-60 attendees) and for approval in May 2024 (≈80 attendees). The GRADE Guidance Group formally approved the approach on May 2, 2024.

What this adds to what is known?
• Extends previous GRADE publications by offering operational steps and decision flows for when NRSIs can complement, replace, or follow RCT evidence.
• Clarifies how domain-level judgments (risk of bias, indirectness, imprecision, etc.) interact when integrating evidence from different study designs.

metadata.section: What this adds to what is known?

documents.page_content:
What is the implication and what should change now?
• Gives guideline developers, HTA agencies, and evidence synthesis teams clear criteria for when integration is appropriate and when it should be avoided.
• Supports transparent and consistent judgments in contexts where evidence comes from mixed study designs.
• Apply the four-step framework to ensure that integrating RCTs and NRSIs is context-driven, methodologically sound, and clearly reported.
• Use the guidance to harmonize methods across decision-making settings and improve trust in recommendations informed by mixed evidence.
imprecision, inconsistency, indirectness, and dissemination bias -would be unfeasible. With 139 potential scenarios for RCTs and 140 for NRSIs, this yields 19,460 possible combinations. This complexity highlights the need for a structured, principle-based approach.
In the following sections, we outline 4 sequential steps to guide GRADE users when integrating RCTs and NRSIs in an evidence synthesis. The process begins with assessing the certainty of each body of evidence (Step 1), accompanied by guiding principles for evaluating relevant GRADE domains in both study designs. Step 2 focuses on determining the congruency of effects between RCTs and NRSIs, while Step 3 describes the decision-making process for comparing the two bodies of evidence across different scenarios, using the CoE from RCTs as the initial anchor point. Finally, Step 4 provides guidance on how to use and present RCT and NRSI findings within GRADE tables. The role of sensitivity analyses in supporting these assessments is also briefly discussed.

documents.page_content:
Step 1: assessing the CoE of both RCTs and NRSI
This guidance applies once GRADE users have determined that a body of NRSI evidence is relevant to the systematic review question and merits full consideration for integration alongside RCTs. This judgment follows the previous GRADE guidance [8], which helps users decide whether both study types address the same patient, intervention, comparison, outcome (PICO) question and are appropriate to consider together within an evidence synthesis. Building on that foundation, the present guidance suggests authors must assess the CoE for both RCTs and NRSIs individually -as high, moderate, low, or very low, according to current GRADE guidance outline in GRADE's official resource, the GRADE Book [12] -and then proceed to evaluate their combined value.
Before advancing in the evaluation of both bodies of evidence and making integration decisions, users should be familiar with the guiding principles developed through our iterative discussions. These principles, specific to each GRADE domain, underpin later steps in this process and inform final judgments about the overall CoE.

documents.page_content:
Guiding principles for evaluating individual GRADE domains in RCTs and NRSI
The principles here outlined should be applied together with the assessment of effect congruency (Step 2) and the decision scenarios anchored in the CoE of RCTs (Step 3). Collectively, these elements provide a coherent framework for determining when and how to integrate evidence from both study designs.
Table 1 presents a concise summary of the guiding principles for each GRADE domain and indicates when pooling may be appropriate. A detailed description of these principles -including rationale, illustrative examples, and extended explanations for each domain -is provided in the Supplementary Material 1 (section 1) to support reader understanding.
We use the term mitigation to describe circumstances in which a downgraded domain in one body of evidence can be reconsidered based on judgments from the other. For example, if RCTs are rated down one level for serious indirectness but NRSIs -without indirectness -show congruent results, the direct evidence from NRSIs may mitigate the indirectness in RCTs, restoring their rating to ''not serious.''

metadata.section: Guiding principles for evaluating individual GRADE domains in RCTs and NRSI

documents.page_content:
Step 2: assessing congruency of effect estimates from RCTs and NRSI
Using NRSIs in the evidence synthesis depends on their value as replacement of RCTs, which is typically limited to cases where NRSI evidence has higher certainty. More commonly, however, is the case where NRSIs serve as complementary or sequential evidence [8,9], requiring an assessment of the degree to which effect estimates from RCTs and NRSIs align. This is a key consideration when integrating RCTs and NRSIs -essentially, whether they would support the same answer to the same PICO question. We refer to this alignment as effect congruency, and its absence as incongruency. Recent studies have applied various methods to assess congruency [4,[13][14][15], ranging from comparisons of point estimates and CIs [16], classifying effects as statistically significant or not [17], conducting interaction tests within subgroup analyses [18], and comparing relative effects through ratios of odds ratios, risk ratios, or risk differences [13,14,19]. Some studies combine multiple approaches to their assessments [15]. Findings from these studies suggest that effect differences between RCTs and NRSIs are less common than initially assumed, and that when discrepancies arise, they may be influenced by factors beyond study design [13,14,19].
Statistical tests for detecting differences between RCT and NRSI estimates may have limited power [20] and do not account for effect magnitudes relative to important benefit or harm thresholds. To integrate uncertainty, magnitude of effects, and CoE, we defined congruency based on absolute effect thresholds and CIs, as informed by recent GRADE guidance and real-world case studies. These developments enable a straightforward determination of thresholds [21][22][23].
The GRADE Evidence to Decision (EtD) framework categorizes effects as trivial or no effect, small, moderate, or large, with corresponding certainty ranges. The Supplementary Material 1 (Figure S1 in section 2) illustrates how thresholds and magnitudes of effects relate in a typical forest plot from an evidence synthesis with meta-analysis.
We use multiple thresholds (6) as in the EtD to highlight how incongruency between a small and a large effect can be as impactful for decision-making as a difference between a trivial and a small (or important) effect.
Congruency is present when both effect absolute estimates and their CIs from RCTs and NRSIs fall within the same effect magnitude range, as seen in Figure 1A-D. Incongruency occurs when absolute effect estimates and CIs are within different effect magnitude ranges (Figure 1E-H). This approach is also adaptable when fewer thresholds are considered, such as when focusing solely on small effects (determine by the minimally important difference). The implication of using fewer thresholds, however, is that effects may erroneously be considered congruent when they are not (Figure 1G). When effect estimates from RCTs or NRSI are affected by imprecision (ie, wide CI's) establishing congruency becomes more challenging. In such cases, we classify the findings as probable congruency or probable incongruency. For example, in Figure 1I the effect estimate from RCTs is confined to the target certainty range of a small effect, while the CI from NRSIs spans the ranges of important benefit, trivial effect, and important harm. Due to the imprecision of the NRSIs estimate, it is unclear whether the 2 bodies of evidence are truly in agreement. Different scenarios of congruency/incongruency between effect estimates of bodies of evidence of RCTs and NRSIs Illustration of 3 groups of scenarios using classic forest plots, with 6 thresholds that define the zones for magnitude of effect (as described in Fig. S1), to reflect congruency or incongruency in effect estimates between RCTs and NRSIs (see main text for details). Scenarios A, B, C, and D are considered congruent. Scenarios E, F, G, and h are incongruent, while scenarios I, J, K, and l represent situations of probable incongruency (I and J) or probable congruency (K and L) due to imprecision (wide CIs). GRADE users may refer to the flowchart in Figure 2 to help distinguish between these classifications. In scenario g, RCTs and NRSI are incongruent. However, if GRADE users consider only one threshold, ie, the threshold of small but important effect (minimally important difference), these will be classified as congruent, but it bears the risk of combining study designs despite wide variation in the study results (small vs large effects). Note that for congruency assessment both RCTs and NRSI require utilization of the same baseline risk to avoid incongruency based on baseline risk differences. GRADE, Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation, NRSI, nonrandomized studies of interventions; RCT, randomized controlled trial.
To support users in making these determinations, we provide a decision-making flowchart, depicted in Figure 2.
When assessing congruency, authors must use absolute effect estimates -not relative effects. Absolute effects are calculated from pooled relative effects and the control arm's baseline risk. However, baseline risks in RCTs may not always be applicable, as they can differ from those in NRSIs due to more restrictive selection criteria. For instance, RCTs may show a lower baseline risk for outcomes like death, while NRSIs reflect those of higher risk populations. In such cases, authors should derive more applicable baseline risks from large, representative observational studies, or their own syntheses, and apply the relative estimates from the RCTs and NRSI to these baseline risks to evaluate congruency [24].
Establishing congruency between RCTs and NRSIs supports their integration and informs how GRADE domains may interact across both bodies of evidence. In contrast, incongruent findings indicate that the 2 sources should not be combined in the decision-making process. Identifying incongruency early helps users avoid unnecessary complexity and instead focus on the body of evidence that offers the higher CoE for the specific PICO.
In the rare instance where RCTs and NRSIs yield incongruent effect estimates but are rated at the same level of certainty, users must apply nuanced judgment within the level of certainty to determine which body of evidence offers more confidence for the specific decision or context. Flowchart for decisions about determining if effects from RCTs and NRSIs are congruent a. If effect estimates and CI's are completely within a range of a magnitude of effect (delimited by thresholds), we can be confident of its congruency. On the other hand, when RCTs and NRSI are completely separated by a threshold we can ascertain that they are incongruent. We use six thresholds to distinguish between trivial, small, moderate, and large effects (for benefits and harms). This simplified approach is also applicable at subsequent decision nodes in the flowchart. b. Imprecision in one or both CIs from RCTs and NRSIs introduces uncertainty when determining effect congruency. In such cases, authors must judge whether the findings are probably congruent or probably incongruent, as defined in the next steps of this flowchart. c. In most scenarios at this step, the CIs will overlap, and the answer to this question will be ''yes.'' However, if a situation arises similar to Figure 1, example j, where overlap is minimal or absent, the answer would be ''no.'' d. When CIs overlap but span into different magnitude-of-effect zones, the congruency of the effect estimates remains uncertain. In this case, the point estimates can help inform whether the scenario is better classified as probably congruent or probably incongruent, although uncertainty will remain. NRSI, nonrandomized studies of interventions; RCT, randomized controlled trial.

metadata.section: Step 2: assessing congruency of effect estimates from RCTs and NRSI

documents.page_content:
Step 3: comparing the CoE of RCTs and NRSIs
This guidance outlines key scenarios anchored in the CoE from RCTs, and the inclusion of NRSIs should be considered in each scenario, depending on their respective CoE. These are summarized in Table 2 and outlines how NRSI may complement, replace, or better remain as a separate body of evidence. Detailed explanations for each scenario are provided in the Supplementary Material 1 (Section 3).
For each scenario, users should also apply the guiding principles previously detailed in Section 4.2.1 and Table 1 to evaluate how NRSIs may contribute to the overall evidence synthesis and certainty.
Note: Exploring the use of both bodies of evidence with sensitivity analyses
Sensitivity analyses based on study design are wellestablished methods for testing the robustness of findings [25,26]. They involve comparing results when including or excluding specific study types (eg, RCTs vs NRSIs) or studies categorized by high vs low RoB, a commonly recommended and used strategy [27]. These approaches are particularly relevant when evaluating the potential limitations of NRSIs when integrating them with RCTs.
Sensitivity or subgroup analyses can also explore differences in other GRADE domains, such as indirectness or inconsistency. To ensure transparency and methodological rigor, all sensitivity analyses should be prespecified in the review protocol and conducted with input from both methodological and content experts.

documents.page_content:
Step 4: decide whether and how to use one or both bodies of evidence
The final step involves determining how to incorporate RCT and NRSI evidence into the overall synthesis and how to present it transparently in GRADE tables. This decision builds on the preceding steps -assessing CoE, evaluating effect congruency, and comparing both bodies of evidence applying domain-specific guiding principles throughout -and reflects the relative contribution of each body of evidence to the overall certainty rating. Depending on their final judgments, users may select among the following 3 presentation strategies:
1. Separate presentation: List RCTs and NRSIs separately in the GRADE tables without a pooled estimate, using 2 The table illustrates overall guidance on how GRADE users may approach integration of RCT and NRSI evidence across varying levels of certainty. Decisions will depend on the interaction of affected individual GRADE domains as described in the text.
rows. This format applies whether results are presented as meta-analyses, narrative syntheses, or both. 2. Separate rows with a pooled estimate: When a pooled estimate is appropriate, include a third row showing combined RCT + NRSI data while retaining the 2 bodies of evidence as distinct subgroups. 3. Single-row presentation: Use one pooled estimate (without subgroups) or include only one body of evidence while describing the other in footnotes.

metadata.section: Step 4: decide whether and how to use one or both bodies of evidence

documents.page_content:
Decisions -putting all together
Figure 3 provides a summary of the full guidance here presented. The general approach for integrating RCTs and NRSIs to inform a review question involves the acknowledgment that NRSIs are relevant to the review question and warrant full consideration for integration alongside RCTs. By examining the guiding principles, GRADE users can evaluate how the affected domains interact across both bodies of evidence, and whether one helps reducing uncertainty in the other.
We emphasize the need to apply the guiding principles (Section 4.2) across all GRADE domains affected and consider sensitivity or subgroup analyses. Selecting the best presentation requires content and methodological expertise to understand interactions between GRADE domains and their impact on evidence synthesis conclusions. These decisions ultimately shape recommendations in guidelines, HTA panels, and other decision-making settings. In the Supplementary Materials 2 and 3 we present 2 worked examples applying this guidance of review questions using both RCTs and NRSIs. Finally, an infographic putting all guidance and explanations together can be found in the Supplementary Materials.

Discussion
We present comprehensive GRADE guidance for integrating NRSIs with RCTs as replacement, complementary, or sequential evidence. Despite its inherent complexity, the approach remains pragmatic, helping GRADE users navigate over 19,000 possible certainty rating scenarios. The process centers on assessing if effects between the 2 bodies Figure 3. Overview of the process for integrating RCT and NRSI evidence using the GRADE approach The figure summarizes the sequential process for integrating randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and nonrandomized studies of interventions (NRSIs) within evidence Infographic Footnotes (a) Consistent with prior guidance from the GRADE Working Group. b, These principles (recommendations) are essential for understanding the complete process and underlying rationale. See the main text and Supplementary Material for detailed explanations and illustrative examples. c, The steps of assessing congruency and CoE may be performed simultaneously. d, In systematic reviews, authors may still present both bodies of evidence separately to maintain transparency; however, only one should inform the conclusions -specifically, the body with the higher CoE, as it provides more trustworthy information for the PICO being evaluated. e, Scenarios in which NRSI evidence is rated as high certainty are exceedingly rare, as fully addressing confounding and other biases in nonrandomized designs is seldom feasible. f, When RCTs are rated as high certainty, additional information from NRSIs is generally unnecessary, since RCT estimates are considered sufficient and reliable for the specific PICO. g, Choose one of the three available GRADE table formats to present evidence profiles and/or summary of findings tables. Add footnotes as appropriate. These options also apply when a narrative synthesis is used instead of a pooled (meta-analytic) estimate. CoE, certainty of evidence; GRADE, Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development, and Evaluation; HTA, health technology assessment; NRSI, nonrandomized study of interventions; PICO, population, intervention, comparison, outcome; RCT, randomized controlled trial; RoB, risk of bias.

documents.page_content:
Strengths and limitations
This official GRADE guidance reflects contributions from experts in GRADE, Cochrane, guidelines, and HTA communities, ensuring both methodological rigor and practical applicability. Its iterative derivation allowed for refinement through nuanced, real-world scenarios, making it adaptable to common challenges in evidence synthesis. Some limitations remain, particularly when RCTs and NRSIs yield incongruent effect estimates yet are rated at the same level of certainty. In such (rare) cases, determining which body of evidence to rely on depends on nuanced assessments within a level of certainty using judgment (eg, if RoB is more important than imprecision) and contextual interpretation. Additionally, the complexity of all possible combinations of certainty ratings and affected domains highlights the impracticality of laying out every scenario individually in this article. We have adopted a pragmatic, principle-based approach to support reviewers now, while also acknowledging that further guidance, decision aids, and large language models (artificial intelligence)-assisted tools are in development to support more consistent and scalable applications.

documents.page_content:
Implications for decision-makers and guideline developers
This GRADE guidance provides a structured approach to integrate evidence from RCTs and NRSIs in evidence syntheses. The guidance applies broadly to various NRSI designs -from large cohorts to other types often inappropriately referred to as real-world evidence -especially useful when RCTs alone are insufficient. By helping users determine when integration is appropriate and when separate consideration is warranted, it promotes more transparent, consistent, and context-relevant decision-making. Future guidance will expand on key aspects that enhance confidence in NRSIs, such as directed acyclic graphs, statistical adjustment methods, assessment of bias direction, and specific NRSI designs. We also plan to address more advanced strategies for summarizing and presenting integrated evidence in GRADE tables and EtD frameworks.

metadata.section: Implications for decision-makers and guideline developers

documents.page_content:
Conclusions
We developed official GRADE guidance for authors incorporating NRSIs alongside RCTs in syntheses of health interventions. This work offers a practical approach to assess congruency, navigate interactions across GRADE domains, and support transparent decision-making when combining evidence from different study designs or deciding to focus on one design. The guidance is expected to improve consistency in how NRSIs are considered, particularly in areas where RCTs alone are insufficient and will be added to the GRADE Book as GRADE's final official resource.
